matter.title: Grace C. Roe v. Maria Q. Fairbanks (interstate custody)
matter.summary: Grace C. Roe, appearing without a lawyer, asks a Maine District Court to modify an existing out-of-state order for one child(ren), raising a threshold UCCJEA question because the other parent is in Massachusetts and the child's home state may be Maine.

fact_pattern.summary: Grace C. Roe, appearing without a lawyer, asks a Maine District Court to modify an existing out-of-state order for one child(ren), raising a threshold UCCJEA question because the other parent is in Massachusetts and the child's home state may be Maine.
fact_pattern.narrative:
Grace C. Roe is a self-represented parent living in Sagadahoc County, Maine. The other parent, Maria Q. Fairbanks, lives in Massachusetts. Around 2025-05-29, the living arrangements changed, and the child has now been in Maine for roughly 15 months by the plaintiff's count.

Because the parents and the child are spread across state lines, the first question is not the merits but jurisdiction: under the Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act, a court must determine the child's 'home state' before it can make an initial custody determination, and no prior custody order exists.

Grace seeks to modify an existing out-of-state order. Proceeding without counsel, Grace needs the court to sort out whether Maine has jurisdiction, whether it must communicate with the other state's court, and what temporary arrangements protect the child in the meantime.
undisputed_facts: The parents live in different states (Sagadahoc County, Maine and Massachusetts).
The child's living arrangements changed on or about 2025-05-29.
The plaintiff is proceeding without an attorney.
disputed_facts: Which state is the child's home state for UCCJEA purposes.
Whether Maine has initial, modification, or only emergency jurisdiction.
Where the child has primarily resided over the past six months.

third_parties.name: Felix B. Poe
third_parties.relevance: Has provided care during the child's time in Maine.

third_parties.name: Rosa M. Greenhill
third_parties.relevance: Can attest to where the child has actually been living and attending school.

exchanges.question: In the last six months, where has the child actually lived?
exchanges.answer: Mostly with me in Maine, about 15 months, but it has gone back and forth since 2025-05-29.

exchanges.question: Where is the other parent now?
exchanges.answer: Maria is in Massachusetts. That is part of the problem; we are in different states.

exchanges.question: Is there any custody order already?
exchanges.answer: Honestly I am not sure it counts, but no prior custody order exists.

exchanges.question: Will you have a lawyer?
exchanges.answer: No, I cannot afford one right now. I am doing this myself with the court's self-help materials.

exchanges.question: What are you asking the court to do?
exchanges.answer: I want to modify an existing out-of-state order and keep things stable for the kids while it gets sorted out.

goals: Establish that Maine has (or does not have) jurisdiction under the UCCJEA before litigating the merits.
Obtain a workable parental rights and contact schedule for the child(ren).
Keep the children stable during the cross-border dispute.
priorities: Getting the jurisdiction question right the first time.
Stability and continuity for the children.
desired_outcomes: A jurisdictional ruling and, if Maine has jurisdiction, an initial or modified order.
Clear temporary arrangements pending a final determination.
non_starters: A ruling that destabilizes the children's schooling and routine.

constraints.budget: Self-represented; cannot afford an attorney and relies on court self-help resources.
constraints.timeline: Wants temporary clarity quickly, before the next school term.

issues.issue: Registration and enforcement of an out-of-state custody order
sub_questions: Must any existing out-of-state order be registered before enforcement?
Is the child present in Maine and is emergency protection necessary?
issues.client_position: Asserts an emergency basis only if the child is at risk.

sub_questions: Should Maine decline in favor of the other state?
Is a proceeding already pending elsewhere?

expert_opinions.opinion: Continuity of the child's schooling and caregivers favors a stable primary residence.

evidence.title: Pediatric and medical records
evidence.description: Continuity-of-care records reflecting the child's primary residence.

evidence.title: Residency and travel log
evidence.description: The plaintiff's record of where the child has lived month to month.
